topic: Sharp limit experiment and a tail-adaptive honest-CI adaptation lower bound for the continuous-instrument optimal-transport PRTE sharp partial-identification bound endpoint, indexed by the propensity-boundary tail exponent alpha; phase transition alpha*=1 separating root-n-regular (Gaussian) from boundary-irregular (non-Gaussian) limits; deliver both the boundary-localized limit law and a two-alpha Le Cam adaptation lower bound (no honest CI adapts across the tail class). Anchor: arXiv:2604.12263 (PRTE partial ID with IV via optimal transport), whose Thm 5.11 gives only a rate for the continuous-instrument bound endpoint, no limit law or CI.

open_problems.open_problem: Derive the sharp boundary-localized local experiment and normalized endpoint limit law for the continuous-instrument OT PRTE sharp bound endpoint as the propensity-boundary tail exponent alpha crosses 1.

source_refs.claim: Frames limited-support PRTE partial identification as a constrained conditional OT problem and gives continuous-instrument nonparametric rates, but not a boundary-localized endpoint limit law or CI.
source_refs.author: Jiyuan Tan, Jose Blanchet, Vasilis Syrgkanis

source_refs.claim: Develops PRTE limit theory with generated nonparametric components for point-identified PRTEs, not sharp OT partial-ID endpoints under boundary-tail irregularity.
source_refs.author: Yuya Sasaki, Takuya Ura

source_refs.claim: Obtains limit theory and confidence bands for nonparametric boundary/frontier functions, showing adjacent boundary laws exist outside the PRTE-OT setting.
source_refs.author: Abdelaati Daouia, Jean-Pierre Florens, Leopold Simar

source_refs.excerpt: prove an impossibility theorem for uniformly adaptive confidence intervals across kappa-neighborhoods crossing 1.
complete the Le Cam / Assouad handle on W_n^lb into a bounded-divergence family

open_problems.why_unsolved: The anchor paper gives a sharp CCOT representation and rates for continuous instruments, while PRTE inference papers handle regular point-identified functionals and frontier/cube-root theories handle different boundary objects. The missing obstacle is the experiment-level localization of the OT endpoint at the thin propensity boundary: the endpoint depends on tail mass, active transport faces, and MTE weights, so existing Gaussian PRTE scores and generic nonregular M-estimator limits do not identify the alpha-indexed normalization or the alpha=1 transition law.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A local asymptotic experiment for DGP sequences with boundary mass P(p near boundary) proportional to t^alpha, plus an explicit normalization and limit process proving a regular Gaussian law on the root-n side, a boundary-irregular non-Gaussian law on the thin-tail side, and a distinct transition law at alpha=1.

writing_exemplar.why: Its motivation already sets up limited IV support, CCOT sharp bounds, and the continuous-instrument rate-only gap.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: limited IV support makes PRTE partially identified -> CCOT recovers sharp distributional bounds -> continuous-instrument endpoints have rates but no boundary-tail limit law

method_exemplars.technique: Localize observations near an estimated boundary/frontier and prove an extreme-value boundary limit with confidence-band calibration.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The PRTE endpoint is also governed by observations near a support boundary, but with the OT endpoint replacing the frontier functional.

method_exemplars.technique: Derive a non-Gaussian argmax limit for a nonsmooth criterion by local empirical-process expansion.
method_exemplars.why_closest: If the active OT face is selected by a local nonsmooth optimization, its boundary law may require an argmax or drifted-process proof rather than a linear influence function.

method_exemplars.technique: Orthogonalize generated propensity and policy components in PRTE asymptotics.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It is the nearest PRTE-specific asymptotic template, even though the desired endpoint is sharp partial-ID and boundary irregular.

open_problems.open_problem: Prove a two-alpha Le Cam lower bound showing that no honest confidence interval for the continuous-instrument OT PRTE endpoint adapts to the alpha-specific oracle length over two propensity-boundary tail classes.

source_refs.claim: Provides sharp OT PRTE bounds and continuous-instrument rates but no honest confidence interval theory or adaptation result across tail exponents.
source_refs.author: Jiyuan Tan, Jose Blanchet, Vasilis Syrgkanis

source_refs.claim: Constructs confidence intervals for partially identified parameters under asymptotically normal lower and upper bound estimators, leaving irregular endpoint regimes outside the template.
source_refs.author: Guido W. Imbens, Charles F. Manski

source_refs.claim: Shows uniformity subtleties for confidence intervals in interval-identified models even when bound estimators are regular.
source_refs.author: Jorg Stoye

source_refs.claim: Uses between-class moduli to characterize sharp expected-length lower bounds for adaptive confidence intervals.
source_refs.author: T. Tony Cai, Mark G. Low

source_refs.excerpt: prove an impossibility theorem for uniformly adaptive confidence intervals across kappa-neighborhoods crossing 1.
Between-class adaptation impossibility in irregular mildly ill-posed no-solution regimes

open_problems.why_unsolved: Partial-ID CI theory is calibrated around regular bound estimators or moment-inequality criteria, while adaptive honest-CI lower bounds are formulated for abstract nonparametric functionals. The obstacle is to construct two PRTE-OT data-generating sequences with different alpha values that are statistically contiguous but whose OT endpoint separation exceeds the shorter alpha-specific oracle length; without that pair, the no-adaptation statement is only an analogy to Cai-Low rather than an econometric theorem.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A two-class Le Cam construction for alpha0 and alpha1 tail classes, with bounded Hellinger/KL distance, an explicit OT endpoint separation calculation, and a lower bound proving every CI honest over both classes has expected length at least the slower-class order on one class.

writing_exemplar.why: It frames adaptation through expected CI length and between-class moduli, exactly the narrative needed for a no-honest-adaptation PRTE result.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: honest coverage constrains uncertainty quantification -> between-class alternatives limit adaptive expected length -> PRTE tail classes require a two-alpha modulus lower bound

method_exemplars.technique: Compute a between-class modulus and convert it into an expected-length lower bound for honest CIs.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The desired PRTE statement is the same logical object, with alpha-tail classes replacing smoothness classes.

method_exemplars.technique: Translate endpoint estimator distributions into confidence intervals for a partially identified scalar interval.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It is the canonical partial-ID CI template that the irregular PRTE endpoint lower bound must exceed or modify.

method_exemplars.technique: Use testing/contiguity obstructions to show honest adaptive confidence regions require restrictions.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The PRTE alpha classes likewise require a testing obstruction to rule out uniformly adaptive honest CIs.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize the active OT transport face and directional derivative of the PRTE sharp endpoint at propensity-boundary laws, including tied or slack active sets that determine the endpoint's local distribution.

source_refs.claim: Derives separable one-dimensional OT formulas for sharp PRTE bounds but leaves continuous-instrument endpoint inference at the level of rates.
source_refs.author: Jiyuan Tan, Jose Blanchet, Vasilis Syrgkanis

source_refs.claim: Shows that standard bootstrap fails for directionally differentiable maps unless stronger differentiability holds, motivating explicit derivative and resampling analysis.
source_refs.author: Zheng Fang, Andres Santos

source_refs.claim: Develops inference for partially identified moment models via regularized OT and bootstrap for directionally differentiable functionals, but not PRTE boundary-tail endpoints.
source_refs.author: Grigory Franguridi, Laura Liu

source_refs.excerpt: uniform weak limits for overlap-constrained lower and upper DTE-bound processes
inference for the multi-IV tightening gain is nonsmooth at slack joint moments and tied single-IV benchmarks
future work here needs a genuinely new correction/calibration rule

open_problems.why_unsolved: Generic max/supremum and directionally differentiable inference results do not by themselves identify the local derivative of a CCOT-derived PRTE endpoint: the active transport face can move with the propensity boundary and with MTE policy weights. Prior CausalSmith OT and MTE attempts flagged weak-limit and nonsmooth-active-set ideas but either left inference out of scope or used invalid algebra, so the concrete active-face derivative needed for a limit law remains unpinned.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A support-price or transport-dual derivative theorem for the PRTE-OT endpoint at boundary-tail laws, including a regime statement for unique, tied, and slack active faces and the induced first-order local limit map.

writing_exemplar.why: It supplies the exact CCOT endpoint map whose missing local geometry is the problem.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: sharp PRTE bounds reduce to separable OT -> endpoint inference requires local sensitivity of the OT value -> active transport faces at propensity boundaries must be characterized

method_exemplars.technique: Derive and estimate the directional derivative of a nonsmooth functional, then calibrate inference when standard bootstrap is invalid.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The PRTE endpoint is plausibly only directionally differentiable at tied or boundary active faces.

method_exemplars.technique: Smooth an OT support-function representation by entropic regularization and prove a CLT/bootstrap result for the regularized value.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It is an OT partial-ID inference template, though the target here is a PRTE endpoint with boundary-tail irregularity.

method_exemplars.arxiv_id_or_doi: classic sharp distribution-bound result; DOI not located
method_exemplars.technique: Use extremal couplings to characterize sharp distributional bounds.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The PRTE-OT endpoint also turns on extremal coupling geometry and active transport plans.

open_problems.open_problem: Build uniformly valid inference for continuous-instrument OT PRTE sharp endpoints when propensity scores, conditional outcome laws, and OT quantile objects are learned flexibly under alpha-dependent boundary irregularity.

source_refs.claim: Motivates orthogonal PRTE scores because first-, second-, and third-stage nuisance estimators otherwise enter the asymptotic distribution in complicated ways.
source_refs.author: Yuya Sasaki, Takuya Ura

source_refs.claim: Develops double/debiased machine learning with Neyman orthogonality and sample splitting for regular treatment and structural parameters.
source_refs.author: Victor Chernozhukov, Denis Chetverikov, Mert Demirer, Esther Duflo, Christian Hansen, Whitney Newey, James Robins

source_refs.claim: Constructs confidence regions for parameter sets in econometric models, but in criterion/moment frameworks rather than OT boundary-tail endpoint functionals.
source_refs.author: Victor Chernozhukov, Han Hong, Elie Tamer

source_refs.claim: Provides calibrated projection confidence intervals with uniform asymptotic coverage for smooth functions of partially identified parameters in moment-inequality models.
source_refs.author: Hiroaki Kaido, Francesca Molinari, Jorg Stoye

open_problems.why_unsolved: Orthogonal scores and cross-fitting are available for regular smooth functionals, and partial-ID projection methods cover many moment-inequality settings. The continuous-instrument OT PRTE endpoint combines estimated conditional distributions, quantile/coupling maps, and a boundary-tail nonsmooth functional; the nuisance products that are second order under alpha>1 may be first order when alpha<1, so standard DML rate conditions do not automatically give honest coverage.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: An orthogonal or locally robust endpoint representation where possible, alpha-indexed nuisance-rate product conditions, and a valid analytic or bootstrap calibration that is uniformly honest over shrinking boundary neighborhoods and distinguishes regular from irregular tail regimes.

writing_exemplar.why: It frames PRTE inference around generated propensity and policy components, which is the setup to mirror before pivoting to sharp OT endpoints.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: PRTE inference uses estimated first-stage objects -> orthogonalization controls nuisance impact in regular cases -> OT sharp endpoints need alpha-dependent nuisance and boundary calibration

method_exemplars.technique: Use Neyman-orthogonal scores and cross-fitting to make nuisance estimation second order.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The endpoint inference target needs an explicit check of which nuisance channels remain second order by alpha regime.

method_exemplars.technique: Calibrate projection confidence intervals for functions of partially identified sets with uniform coverage.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The PRTE endpoint is a scalar function of a sharp identified set, but with OT and boundary-tail complications.

method_exemplars.technique: Invert criterion-based tests to obtain confidence regions for partially identified parameter sets.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It supplies the classical uniform partial-ID inference comparator that the OT endpoint method must improve or specialize.

literature_map:
1. Heckman-Vytlacil MTE/PRTE makes policy effects weighted averages over latent resistance, with propensity support as the identification bottleneck.
2. Mogstad-Santos-Torgovitsky and Han-Yang treat policy-relevant partial identification as extrapolation from limited IV variation; Han-Yang emphasizes infinite-dimensional LP computation.
3. Marx gives sharp analytic latent-index bounds using full marginal distributional content, beyond mean/MTR relaxations.
4. Tan-Blanchet-Syrgkanis 2026 moves the PRTE partial-ID thread to constrained conditional OT, deriving sharp closed-form bounds and continuous-instrument rates.
5. The explicit anchor gap is inferential: for continuous instruments, the anchor gives rates but not endpoint limit laws, local experiments, or honest CIs.
6. Sasaki-Ura is the closest PRTE inference exemplar, but targets point-identified PRTE with orthogonal scores rather than sharp partial-ID OT endpoints.
7. Imbens-Manski and Stoye solve canonical CIs for interval-identified parameters with asymptotically normal bound estimators; boundary-irregular endpoints fall outside that template.
8. Chernozhukov-Hong-Tamer, Andrews-Soares, and Kaido-Molinari-Stoye cover partial-ID and moment-inequality inference, but not OT tail-localized endpoint functionals.
9. Daouia-Florens-Simar boundary/frontier theory and Kim-Pollard cube-root asymptotics supply adjacent non-Gaussian boundary-law techniques.
10. Adaptive honest CI theory from Cai-Low and Robins-van der Vaart suggests a no-adaptation lower-bound program across alpha tail classes.
11. Weak-ID econometrics from Staiger-Stock, Stock-Wright, and Andrews-Cheng supplies the uniformity mindset, but PRTE has weak support/thin boundary mass rather than a finite-dimensional concentration parameter.
12. OT causal and partial-ID papers such as Ober-Reynolds and Fan-Pass-Shi show transport duality is increasingly central, but their root-n or moment-model settings do not resolve alpha-indexed boundary irregularity.
13. The main tension is that sharpness uses full distributional OT geometry, while inference must handle estimated propensity support, tail mass, nonsmooth quantiles, and moving active faces.
14. The most promising open path is to write the boundary-localized limit experiment first, then derive alpha-specific endpoint laws and a two-alpha honest-CI lower bound.
prior_proposal_map:
1. Exact qid stat_prte_ot_boundary_adaptation currently contains only state/pipeline initialization and no direct proposal text.
2. stat_ate_overlap_decay was the strongest adjacent Stat source: it names one-sided propensity-tail kappa, kappa=1 behavior, a Le Cam/Assouad lower-bound gap, and an unpursued adaptive-CI impossibility seed.
3. pid_overlap_tail_phase is a negative partial-ID tail prior: its alpha-width idea failed by collapsing to Manski missing-mass/regular-variation bookkeeping.
4. pid_ot_dte_marginal supplied OT partial-ID endpoint/path-set material, PRTE-as-conditional-OT adjacency, and an unproved weak-limit seed for estimated overlap-constrained bounds.
5. pid_mte_multi_iv supplied PRTE/MTE support-function and nonsmooth-inference motifs, but its bounded-violation algebra was rejected as ill-posed.
6. pid_bound_intersection_inference supplied the generic active-set/supremum endpoint-inference warning: standard CLR/Fang-Santos theory already covers many contact-phase claims.
7. stat_npiv_nosol_modulus supplied the between-class modulus/adaptive honest-inference template, while warning that exact reductions can erase novelty.
8. stat_dose_response_minimax supplied reusable continuous-dose Le Cam witness machinery and an open smoothness-frontier/adaptive-CI implication.
